TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
CHAPTER 1.03
INTERPRETATION ORDINANCE
and Subsidiary Legislation
Revised Edition
showing the law as at 31 December 2014
This is a revised edition of the law, prepared by the Law Revision Commissioner under the authority
of the Revised Edition of the Laws Ordinance 1997.
This edition contains a consolidation of the following laws—

INTERPRETATION ORDINANCE 3
Ordinance 5 of 1946 .. in force 13 April 1946
Amended by Ordinances: 23 of 1968

8 of 1997 .. in force 25 September 1997

CHANGE OF TITLE NOTICES – Section 47 25

CHAPTER 1.03
INTERPRETATION ORDINANCE
(Ordinances 5 of 1946, 23 of 1968,
10 of 1974, 12 of 1978 and 8 of 1997)
Commencement
[13 April 1946]
Short Title
1. This Ordinance may be cited as the Interpretation Ordinance.
Terms used in the Constitution to have same meaning in Ordinances
2. In this Ordinance or in any other Ordinance, unless the context otherwise
requires, any expression not otherwise defined shall have the meaning given to
that expression, if used therein, in the Constitution.
Interpretation of terms applicable generally
3. In this Ordinance and in all Ordinances, proclamations, regulations, rules,
by-laws, orders, directions, notices, forms and other instruments of a public
character relating to the Islands, now in force or hereafter to be made, the
following words and expressions shall have the meanings hereby assigned to
them respectively, unless there is something in the subject or context inconsistent
with such construction or unless it is therein otherwise expressly provided—
“Act” used with reference to legislation includes an Act of the Parliament of the
United Kingdom;
“act” used with reference to an offence or civil wrong, includes a series of acts,
and words which refer to acts done extend to illegal omissions;
“affidavit” includes any document in relation to which an affirmation or
declaration has been made by any person allowed by law to affirm or to
declare instead of swearing;
“British possession” means any Dominion or any British Colony or Protectorate,
or any protected state, or any territory in respect of which a mandate or trust
is being exercised by Her Majesty’s Government in the United Kingdom or
the Government of any part of Her Majesty’s Dominions;
“Christian name” means any name prefixed to a surname, whether received in
Christian baptism or otherwise;
“coin” means any coin legally current in the Islands;
“Collector of Customs”, “Customs Officer” or “Officer of Customs” have the
meanings respectively given to those terms in the Customs Ordinance;
© Crown Copyright 2014

8 CAP. 1.03 Interpretation L

“commencement” used with reference to an Ordinance means the time at which
the Ordinance comes into operation;
“committed for trial” used in relation to any person means committed to prison
with the view of being tried before a Judge and jury by any authority having
power to commit a person to prison with a view to his trial, and includes a
person who is admitted to bail upon a recognizance to appear and take his
trial before a Judge and jury;
“common law” means the common law of England;
“Commonwealth” means the British Commonwealth and includes all the
sovereign states which are members thereof and their dependencies and any
territories held in trust by any such state;
“Consul” or “consular officer” includes Consul-General, Consul, Vice-Consul,
Consular Agent and any person, for the time being authorised to discharge
the duties of a Consul-General, Consul, or Vice-Consul;
“contravene”, with its grammatical variations and cognate expressions, in
relation to any requirement or condition prescribed in any Ordinance, or in
any permit, licence, or other authority, granted under any Ordinance,
includes a failure to comply with that requirement or condition;
“court” means any court of the Islands of competent jurisdiction;
“court of summary jurisdiction” means the Magistrate exercising statutory
summary jurisdiction; (Amended by Ord. 10 of 1974)
“Crown Agents” means the persons for the time being acting as Crown Agents
for Overseas Governments in England, or any of them;
“currency” means any money which for the time being is legal tender in the
Islands, and any reference to any coin, bank or other currency note or sum
of money means a coin, bank or other currency note or sum of money in
such currency; (Inserted by Ord. 10 of 1974)
“dental practitioner” means any person registered as qualified to practise
dentistry under the Health Practitioners Ordinance; (Amended by Ord. 12 of
1978)
“Dominion” means a Dominion as defined in the Statute of Westminster, 1931*;
“financial year” means the twelve months ending the 31st March in any year;
“folio” means seventy-two words;
“Gazette” or “Government Gazette” or “Turks Islands Gazette” means the Turks
and Caicos Islands Gazette published by order of the Government and
includes any Supplements thereto and any Gazette Extraordinary so
published;
“Gazetted” means published in the Gazette;
*
22 George 5 c. 4

& Interpretation CAP. 1.03 9
Revision Date: 31 Dec 2014
“Government” means the Government of the Islands;
“Government Notice” means an announcement made in the Gazette by or with
the authority of the Governor;
“Government Printer” means the Government Printer of the Islands and includes
any printer purporting to be the printer authorised by the Government to
print Ordinances and subsidiary legislation;
“house” includes every messuage, part of a messuage, house, part of a house,
building, or other construction, whether wholly or in part above or below
the surface of the ground, inhabited or occupied either by day or by night by
man, whether beneficially or otherwise, or intended to be so inhabited or
occupied;
“the Islands” means the Turks and Caicos Islands; (Inserted by Ord. 8 of 1997)
“Judge” means a Judge of the Supreme Court and includes the Chief Justice;
“Justice” or “Justice of the Peace” means a person appointed to be a Justice of
the Peace under the provisions of the Magistrate’s Court Ordinance;
“land” and “premises” includes all tenements or hereditaments, and also all
messuages, houses, buildings, or other constructions, whether the property
of Her Majesty, her heirs or successors, or of any corporation, or of any
private individual, except where there are words to exclude houses and
other buildings;
“medical practitioner” means any person registered as qualified to practise
medicine under the Health Practitioners Ordinance; (Amended by Ord. 12 of
1978)
“oath” includes affirmation or declaration in the case of persons by law allowed
to affirm or declare instead of swearing;
“to occupy”, with its grammatical variations and cognate expressions, includes,
in addition to its ordinary signification, to use, inhabit, possess or enjoy the
premises in respect whereof that verb is used, otherwise than as a mere
servant and for the mere purpose of the care, custody, and charge, thereof;
“Ordinance” includes any enactment, in force in the Islands, of the Legislature of
the Bahamas, the Legislature of Jamaica or the Legislature of the Islands by
whatever name called, and any subsidiary legislation made under any such
enactment;
“person” includes any corporation, either aggregate or sole, and any club,
society, association, or other body, of one or more persons;
“police officer” means any person appointed to be a police officer, non-
commissioned officer, constable or district constable under the provisions
of any law for the time being in force which provides for the constitution of
a Police Force;
“prescribed” means prescribed by the Ordinance in which the word occurs or any
subsidiary legislation made thereunder, and, in relation to any subsidiary
legislation, where no other authority is empowered in that behalf in the
Ordinance, means prescribed by the Governor in Cabinet;

“property” includes money, goods, things in action, land and every description of
property, whether real or personal; also obligations, easements and every
description of estate, interest and profit, present or future, vested or
contingent, arising out of or incident to property as above defined;
“public holiday” means any day which, under the provisions of any Ordinance
for the time being in force is, or is declared to be, or is appointed by the
Governor by notification in the Gazette or is proclaimed as a public holiday;
“public place” includes every public highway, street, road, square, court, alley,
lane, bridleway, pathway, parade, wharf, jetty, quay, bridge, public garden,
or open space, and every theatre, place of public entertainment of any kind,
or other place of general resort, admission to which is obtained by payment
or to which the public have access;
“Public Seal” means the Public Seal of the Islands;
“recorded”, used with reference to a document, means registered under the
provisions of the Ordinance applicable to the registration of such document;
“Revenue Officer” means any officer employed in or about the business of any
branch of the public revenue and includes any Customs Officer;
“rules of court” when used in relation to any court, means rules made by the
authority having for the time being power to make rules or orders regulating
the practice and procedure of such court;
“Secretary of State” means one of Her Majesty’s Principal Secretaries of State
for the time being;
“sell”, with its grammatical variations and cognate expressions, includes
exchange and barter;
“ship” means every description of vessel used in navigation not exclusively
propelled by oars;
“sign”, with its grammatical variations and cognate expressions, with reference
to a person who is unable to write his name, includes “mark”;
“Statute” includes an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom;
“street” or “road” includes any public highway, street, road, thoroughfare,
square, court, alley, lane, bridleway, pathway, parade, passage or open
place used or frequented by the public, or to which the public have or are
permitted to have access;
“subsidiary legislation” means any proclamation, rule, regulation, order, notice,
by-law, notification, direction, form or other instrument made under any
Ordinance or other lawful authority and having legislative effect;
“summary” or “summarily” with reference to the trial of an offence means a trial
in the Magistrate’s Court in the exercise of its summary jurisdiction in
accordance with the provisions of the Magistrate’s Court Ordinance;
“summary conviction” means a conviction recorded after a summary trial;
“Supreme Court” means the Supreme Court of the Turks and Caicos Islands
constituted under the Supreme Court Ordinance;

Revision Date: 31 Dec 2014
“swear”, with its grammatical variations and cognate expressions, includes to
affirm or to declare in the case of any person allowed by law to affirm, or
declare instead of swearing;
“Treasurer” means the Permanent Secretary, Finance;
“Treasury” means the Treasury of the Islands;
“United Kingdom” means the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern
Ireland;
“vessel” includes any ship, boat, lighter, or other floating craft, used for transport
by water;
“voluntary declaration” or “statutory declaration” if made—
(a) in the United Kingdom or any British possession, means a
declaration made before a justice of the peace, notary public, or
other person having authority therein under any law for the time
being in force to take or receive a declaration;
(b) in any other place outside the Islands, means a declaration made
before a British Consul or Vice-Consul, or before any person
having authority under any Act of the Parliament of the United
Kingdom for the time being in force to take or receive a
declaration;
“will” includes codicil;
“writing”, with its grammatical variations and cognate expressions, includes
printing, lithography, type-writing, photography, and other modes of
representing or reproducing words or figures in visible form;
“year” and “month” mean respectively a year or a month reckoned according to
the British calendar.
General interpretation of gender and number
4. In this Ordinance and in all Ordinances and other instruments of a public
character relating to the Islands now in force or hereafter to be made, unless
there is something in the subject or context inconsistent with such construction,
or unless it is therein otherwise expressly provided—
(a) words importing the masculine gender include females; and
(b) words in the singular include the plural, and words in the plural
include the singular.
References to public officers by title of office
5. A reference in any Ordinance to any public officer by the usual title of his
office shall, if there be such an office customarily in the Islands and unless the
contrary intention appears, be read and construed as referring to the person for
the time being holding or carrying out the duties of that office in the Islands.
© Crown Copyright 2014
Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.
Printed under Authority by
The Regional Law Revision Centre Inc.

12 CAP. 1.03 Interpretation L
C

Revision Date: 31 Dec 2014
References to the Crown
6. In any Ordinance references to the Sovereign reigning at the time of the
passing of the Ordinance or to the Crown shall, unless the contrary intention
appears, be construed as references to the Sovereign for the time being.
References to time means standard time: power to declare standard time
7. Whenever any expression of time occurs in any Ordinance, deed, or other
legal instrument, the time referred to shall, unless it is otherwise expressly
provided, be held to signify the standard time adopted for the Islands; and for
such purpose the Governor in Cabinet may by order declare the standard time for
the Islands.
Computation of time
8. In computing time for the purpose of any Ordinance unless the contrary
intention appears—
(a) a period of days from the happening of an event or the doing of
any act or thing shall be deemed to be exclusive of the day in
which the event happens or the act or thing is done;
(b) if the last day of the period is a Sunday or a public holiday (which
days are in this section referred to as excluded days) the period
shall include the next following day, not being an excluded day;
(c) when any act or proceeding is directed or allowed to be done or
taken on a certain day, then if that day happens to be an excluded
day, the act or proceeding shall be considered as done or taken in
due time if it is done or taken on the next day afterwards, not
being an excluded day;
(d) when an act or proceeding is directed or allowed to be done or
taken within any time not exceeding six days, excluded days shall
not be reckoned in the computation of the time.
Provisions where no time prescribed
9. Where no time is prescribed or allowed within which anything shall be
done, such thing shall be done with all convenient speed, and as often as the
prescribed occasion arises.
Measurement of distances
10. In the measurement of any distance for the purpose of any Ordinance, that
distance shall, unless the contrary intention appears, be measured in a straight
line on a horizontal plane.
References to the number of a line
11. A reference in any Ordinance to the number of a line of any section
means such line in the latest official printed copy of such Ordinance at the time
of the passing of the Ordinance containing such reference.
© Crown Copyright 2014

Definitions subject to context and to apply to Subsidiary Legislation, etc.
12. (1) Where expressions are defined in any Ordinance, such expressions
shall have the meanings assigned to them, unless there is anything in the subject
or context repugnant to or inconsistent with such meaning.
(2) Where expressions defined in any Ordinance are used in any
subsidiary legislation or instrument made under such Ordinance, such
expressions shall have the respective meanings assigned to them by the
Ordinance, unless there is anything in the subject or context repugnant to or
inconsistent with such meaning.
Commencement and Citation of Ordinances
Authenticated copy and assent to Ordinances
13. (1) A copy of every Ordinance which may hereafter be passed by the
House of Assembly shall immediately after it is so passed be printed by the
Government Printer on vellum or some durable paper and shall, if such be the
case, be authenticated both by the Governor and Clerk to the Assembly as being
a true and correct copy of the Ordinance which passed.
(2) Where the Governor assents to any Ordinance so passed, his assent
shall be made on the authenticated copy which shall then be forwarded to the
Registrar of Deeds for the purposes of record.
Assent to and publication of Ordinances
14. In the case of every Ordinance which may hereafter be passed in the
Islands, the Governor, on assenting thereto, shall cause a notification of such
assent to be published in the Gazette, and every Ordinance shall be published by
Government Notice on or before the date on which it comes into operation:
Provided that if printed copies of any Ordinance are not available for
publication on or before such date it shall suffice, pending such publication, if it
be notified in the Gazette that copies of such Ordinance are available at
Government Offices or appointed public places for inspection by members of the
public.
(Inserted by Ord. 10 of 1974)
Commencement of Ordinance on publication of assent, etc.
15. (1) Every Ordinance shall, unless it is otherwise therein expressly
provided, come into operation on the day of the publication of the notification of
assent.
(2) The date on which an Ordinance comes into operation, whether under
the provisions of this section or according to the express provisions contained in
the Ordinance, shall be written on the original Ordinance and on all copies
thereof in some convenient place near the heading thereof.
© Crown Copyright 2014
Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.

14 CAP. 1.03 Interpretation L

Moment when Ordinance or Subsidiary Legislation comes into operation
16. Where any Ordinance, or part of an Ordinance, or any subsidiary
legislation made thereunder comes into operation on a particular day, it shall be
deemed to have come or shall come into operation immediately on the expiration
of the day next preceding such day.
Ordinances to be public and judicially noticed
17. Every Ordinance shall be a public Ordinance and shall be judicially
noticed as such, unless the contrary is expressly provided by the Ordinance.
Sections to be substantive enactments
18. Every section of an Ordinance shall have effect as a substantive
enactment without introductory words.
Mode of citing Ordinances
19. When any Ordinance is referred to, it shall be sufficient for all purposes to
cite such Ordinance either by the short title (if any) by which it is made citable,
or by the year in which it was made and its number among the Ordinances of that
year, or in the case of a revised edition of the Ordinances issued under any
Ordinance providing for the issue of a revised edition, by its short title or its
chapter number; and the reference may in all cases be made according to the
copies of Ordinances printed by the Government Printer.
Collective titles
20. If it is provided that any Ordinance may, as to the whole or any part
thereof, be cited—
(a) with any other Ordinance to form a group of Ordinances; or
(b) with any group of Ordinances,
that group shall be construed as including that Ordinance or part, and the
collective title of the group shall state the year of the first Ordinance in the group
and the year of the last Ordinance in the group; and as often as any subsequent
Ordinance is added to that group the year in which the subsequent Ordinance is
passed shall be substituted for the last year of the group.
Provisions as to Repeals
Repeal of a repealing enactment
21. Where an Ordinance, whether before or after the commencement of this
Ordinance, repeals a repealing enactment, it shall not be construed as reviving
any enactment previously repealed, unless words are added reviving that
enactment. (Amended by Ord. 10 of 1974)
© Crown Copyright 2014
Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.
Printed under Authority by
The Regional Law Revision Centre Inc.

Repeal and substitution
22. Where an Ordinance repeals wholly or partially any former Ordinance
and substitutes provisions for the Ordinance repealed, the repealed Ordinance
shall remain in force until the substituted provisions come into operation.
Effect of repeal
23. (1) Where any Ordinance repeals and re-enacts, with or without
modification, any provision of any Ordinance in force, references in any other
Ordinance to the provision so repealed shall, unless the contrary intention
appears, be construed as references to the provision so re-enacted.
(2) Where any Ordinance repeals any other enactment then unless the
contrary intention appears, the repeal shall not—
(a) revive anything not in force or existing at the time at which the
repeal takes effect; or
(b) affect the previous operation of any enactments so repealed or
anything duly done or suffered under any enactment so repealed;
or
(c) affect any right, privilege, obligation, or liability, acquired,
accrued, or incurred, under any enactment so repealed; or
(d) affect any penalty, fine, forfeiture, or punishment, incurred in
respect of any offence committed against any enactment so
repealed; or
(e) affect any investigation, legal proceeding or remedy, in respect of
any such right, privilege, obligation, liability, penalty, fine,
forfeiture or punishment, as aforesaid; and any such investigation,
legal proceeding or remedy, may be instituted, continued or
enforced, and any such penalty, fine, forfeiture or punishment
may be imposed as if the repealing Ordinance had not been
passed.
Effect of repeal and re-enactment of section empowering the making of
Subsidiary Legislation
24. Where any section conferring a power to make subsidiary legislation is
repealed (whether before or after the coming into operation of this Ordinance)
and other provisions, which include a power to make subsidiary legislation, are
substituted therefor, then all subsidiary legislation made under the repealed
section, and in force at the time of such repeal, shall continue good and valid as
if made under the substituted provisions in so far as they are not inconsistent
with the Ordinance as amended or, if that Ordinance is repealed and replaced,
with the repealing Ordinance.

Provisions as to Subsidiary Legislation
Provisions as to making of Subsidiary Legislation
25. Where an Ordinance confers power on any authority to make or issue
subsidiary legislation, the following provisions shall, unless the contrary
intention appears, have effect with reference to the making, issue and operation
of such subsidiary legislation—
(a) the subsidiary legislation may be at any time amended, varied,
suspended, rescinded or revoked by the same authority and in the
same manner by and in which it was made;
(b) the subsidiary legislation may provide in respect of a breach of
any of the provisions thereof that the offender shall, unless the
Ordinance otherwise provides, be liable to such fine not
exceeding $1,000, or to such term of imprisonment not exceeding
three months, or to both such fine and imprisonment, as may be
therein prescribed; (Amended by Ord. 10 of 1974)
(c) where any Ordinance confers power on any authority to make
subsidiary legislation for any general purpose and also for any
special purposes incidental thereto, the enumeration of the special
purposes shall not be deemed to derogate from the generality of
the powers conferred with reference to the general purpose;
(d) no subsidiary legislation shall be inconsistent with the provisions
of any Ordinance;
(e) any breach of any subsidiary legislation may, unless the
Ordinance otherwise provides, be prosecuted in a summary
manner;
(f) any reference in any subsidiary legislation to “the Ordinance”
shall be read and construed as meaning the Ordinance conferring
the powers to make or issue such subsidiary legislation.
Commencement and proof of Subsidiary Legislation
26. (1) All subsidiary legislation made under any Ordinance or other lawful
authority and having legislative effect shall be published in the Gazette and
unless it be otherwise provided shall take effect and come into operation as law
on the date of such publication.
(2) The production of a copy of the Gazette containing any subsidiary
legislation shall be prima facie evidence in all courts and for all purposes of the
due making and tenor of such subsidiary legislation.
Acts done under Subsidiary Legislation deemed done under Ordinance
27. An act shall be deemed to be done under an Ordinance or by virtue of the
power conferred by an Ordinance or in pursuance or execution of the powers of,
or under the authority of, an Ordinance if it is done under or by virtue of or in
pursuance of any subsidiary legislation made or issued under any power
contained in such Ordinance.

& Interpretation CAP. 1.03 17

Provisions as to Powers
Exercise of powers between passing and commencement of Ordinance
28. Where any Ordinance is not to come into operation immediately on the
passing thereof, and confers powers to make any appointment, or to make, grant
or issue any subsidiary legislation or instruments, or to do any other thing for the
purpose of the Ordinance, that power may, unless the contrary intention appears,
be exercised at any time after the passing of the Ordinance, so far as may be
necessary or expedient for the purpose of bringing the Ordinance into operation
at the date of the commencement thereof, subject to the restriction that any
subsidiary legislation or instruments made, granted or issued under the power
shall not, unless the contrary intention appears in the Ordinance or the subsidiary
legislation or instruments, is or are necessary for bringing the Ordinance into
operation, have any effect until the Ordinance comes into operation.
Construction of provisions as to exercise of powers, etc.
29. (1) Where any Ordinance confers a power or imposes a duty, then, unless
the contrary intention appears, the power may be exercised and the duty shall be
performed from time to time as occasion requires.
(2) Where any Ordinance confers a power or imposes a duty on the
holder of an office, as such, then, unless the contrary intention appears, the
power may be exercised and the duty shall be performed by the holder for the
time being of the office or by a person appointed to act for him.
Power to appoint includes power to suspend or dismiss
30. Where by or under any Ordinance a power to make any appointment is
conferred, then, unless the contrary intention appears, the authority having power
to make the appointment shall also have power to remove, suspend, re-appoint or
reinstate any person appointed in exercise of the power.
Power to fill vacancy temporarily
31. Where by or under any Ordinance, any powers are conferred or any duties
are imposed upon a public officer, the Governor may direct, if from any cause
the office of such public officer is vacant or if during any period, owing to
absence or inability to act from illness or any other cause, such public officer is
unable to exercise the powers or perform the duties of his office, that such
powers shall be had and may be exercised and such duties shall be performed by
the person named by, or by the public officer holding the office designated by,
the Governor; and thereupon such person or public officer, during any such
period, shall have and may exercise such powers and shall perform such duties,
subject to such conditions, exceptions and qualifications as the Governor may
direct.

Appointment to public boards, etc.
32. Where by or under any Ordinance there is power to appoint persons as
members of any public board, commission, corporation, committee or similar
public body, then unless the contrary intention appears, that power shall include
a power to appoint a member to be a Chairman, or acting Chairman in the
absence of the Chairman, or persons as alternative or substitute members in the
absence from any cause of any substantive member.
Powers to appoint by official designation
33. Any appointment made by or under any Ordinance of a person to exercise
any powers, perform any duties or to be a member of any public board,
commission, corporation, committee or similar public body, unless the contrary
intention appears, may be made in favour of the person for the time being
holding any public office by reference to that office by its usual designation, and
thereupon the person for the time being holding such office shall have and may
exercise such powers, perform such duties or be a member of such public board,
commission, corporation, committee or similar body.
Powers of public boards etc., to regulate procedure
34. Where any public board, commission, corporation, committee or similar
public body is established by or under any Ordinance then, unless the contrary
intention appears, such board, commission, corporation, committee or similar
public body may regulate its own procedure by standing orders and its power
and authority shall not be affected by—
(a) any vacancy in the membership thereof; or
(b) any defect subsequently discovered in the appointment or
qualification of a person purporting to be a member thereof.
Declaration of pecuniary interest
35. A member of any public board, commission, corporation, committee or
similar public body who, directly or indirectly, has a pecuniary interest in any
matter under consideration by such public board, commission, corporation,
committee or similar public body, shall be bound to declare such interest and
shall not participate in any vote regarding such matter.
Construction of enabling words
36. Where in any Ordinance power is given to any person to do or enforce the
doing of any act or thing, all such powers shall be understood to be also given as
are reasonably necessary to enable the person to do or enforce the doing of the
act or thing.

Provisions as to Orders in Council,
Acts of Parliament and Jamaica Laws
Reference in Ordinance to provision of Order in Council, Act of
Parliament or Jamaica Law
37. Where in any Ordinance reference is made to any provision of an Order in
Council, Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom or Jamaica Law and that
provision is subsequently repealed and re-enacted without substantial
modification, the reference in such Ordinance to the provision of the Order in
Council, Act or Law so repealed shall, if the context so requires and unless the
contrary intention appears, be construed as a reference to the provisions so re-
enacted.
Acts of Parliament and Jamaica Laws to be read with necessary
modifications
38. Whenever any Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom or Jamaica
Law is extended or applied to the Islands, such Act or Law shall be read with
such formal alterations as to names, localities, courts, officers, persons, moneys,
penalties and otherwise as may be necessary to make it applicable to the circum-
stances.
Provisions as to Penalties
Statement of penalty means maximum penalty
39. Where any fine or penalty is imposed by or under the authority of any
Ordinance it shall be implied that the amount of such fine or penalty is the
maximum amount; and where by any Ordinance any person may be sentenced to
any term of imprisonment it shall be implied that such term of imprisonment is
the maximum term.
Penalty at foot of section indicates maximum penalty for contravention
of section
40. Where in any Ordinance any fine, penalty or term of imprisonment is set
out at the foot of any section it shall indicate that any contravention of the
section, whether by act or omission, shall be an offence against that Ordinance
and shall, unless the contrary intention appears, be punishable by a fine, penalty
or term of imprisonment not exceeding the amount or term stated.
Payment of portion of penalty by direction of Governor
41. Subject to the express provisions of any Ordinance, where any fine or
penalty is imposed by or under the authority of any Ordinance, every such fine
or penalty shall be payable into the general revenues of the Islands, but the
Governor may direct the payment to any aggrieved person, or to any person
whose information or evidence has led to the conviction of the offender or to the
recovery of the fine or penalty, of such proportion of the fine or penalty as he
may think fit.

Disposal of forfeits
42. (1) Where under any Ordinance any animal or thing is adjudged by any
court or other authority to be forfeited, it shall, unless the contrary is otherwise
provided or unless it is expressed by law to be forfeited to any person, be
forfeited to the Crown, and the net proceeds thereof, if it is ordered by competent
authority to be sold, shall be paid into the general revenues of the Islands, unless
other provision is made.
(2) Nothing in this section shall affect any provision in any Ordinance
whereby any portion of any fine or forfeit or of the proceeds of any forfeit is
expressed to be recoverable by any person or may be granted by any authority to
any person.
Imposition of penalty not to bar civil action
43. The imposition of a penalty or fine by any Ordinance, in the absence of
express provision to the contrary, shall not relieve any person from liability to
answer for damages to a person injured.
Provisions as to Offences
Provision as to offences under two or more laws
44. Where any act or omission constitutes an offence under two or more
Ordinances, or both under an Ordinance and under any other law which applies
to the Islands, the offender shall, unless the contrary intention appears, be liable
to be prosecuted and punished under either or any of those Ordinances or under
such other law, but shall not be liable to be punished twice for the same offence.
Attempt to commit an offence to be deemed an offence
45. A provision which constitutes an offence shall, unless the contrary
intention appears, be deemed to provide also that an attempt to commit such
offence shall be an offence against such provision, punishable as if the offence
itself has been committed.
Remission of penalties
46. The Governor may, in the name of Her Majesty, remit in whole or in part
any sum of money which under any Ordinance may be imposed as a penalty,
fine or forfeiture on a convicted offender although such money may be in whole
or in part payable to some person other than the Crown, and to extend the Royal
mercy to any person who may be imprisoned for non-payment of any sum of
money so imposed, although such sum may be in whole or in part payable to
some person other than the Crown.

Miscellaneous
Change in title of public office
47. Whenever any change in the title of any public office occurs, the
Governor, if occasion requires, may by notice in the Gazette declare that such
change of title shall take effect from a date specified in such notice, and, with
effect from such date, any reference in any Ordinance to the former title of such
office, shall be read and construed as a reference to that office by such new title
as the Governor may declare in such notice.
Meaning of service by post
48. Where any Ordinance authorises or requires any document to be served
by post, whether the expression “serve”, “give” or any other expression is used,
then, unless a contrary intention appears, the service shall be deemed to be
effected by properly addressing, prepaying and posting a letter containing the
document, and, unless the contrary is proved, to have been effected at the time at
which the letter would be delivered in the ordinary course of post.
Signification of orders of Governor
49. Where power is given to the Governor or to the Governor in Cabinet to
make subsidiary legislation, it shall be sufficient, unless it is otherwise
expressed, for such subsidiary legislation to be signified under the hand of the
Cabinet Secretary1, as the case may be:
Provided that any proclamation, warrant, or other instrument issued under
the Public Seal, shall be issued under the hand of the Governor himself.
Power of majority
50. Save as is otherwise expressly provided by any Ordinance, whenever any
act or thing is required to be done by more than two persons, a majority of them
may do it.
Division of Ordinance into Parts, etc.
51. Where any Ordinance is divided into Parts, Titles, or other divisions the
fact and particulars of such division shall, with or without express mention
thereof in any Ordinance, be taken notice of in all courts and for all other
purposes whatsoever.
Construction of reference to Ordinance, section, etc.
52. (1) Where in any Ordinance there is a reference to a Section, Part,
Chapter or Schedule by number or letter only, and not in conjunction with the
title or short title of any other Ordinance, such reference shall be construed as a
reference to the Section, Part, Chapter or Schedule of that number or letter con-
tained in the Ordinance in which such reference occurs.
1 NOTE - Section 37 of the Constitution was suspended by the Turks and Caicos Islands Constitution (Interim
Amendment) Order 2009, S.I. 2009 No. 701 (U. K.).

(2) Where in any section of an Ordinance there is a reference to a
subsection, paragraph or sub-paragraph or other division by number or letter
only, and not in conjunction with the number of any section of that or any other
Ordinance, such reference shall be construed as a reference to the subsection,
paragraph, sub-paragraph or other division of that number in the section in which
such reference occurs.
(3) The provisions of subsections (1) and (2) shall apply mutatis mutandis
to subsidiary legislation.
(4) Any reference to an Ordinance in any Ordinance shall include a
reference to any subsidiary legislation made under the Ordinance to which
reference is made.
(Inserted by Ord. 10 of 1974)
Construction of preamble and Schedules of Ordinance
53. (1) The preamble of any Ordinance may be referred to for assistance in
explaining the scope and object of the Ordinance.
(2) Every Schedule or Table to any Ordinance, or part of any Ordinance,
shall, together with any notes thereto, be construed and have effect as part of the
Ordinance.
Deviations from forms
54. Save as is otherwise expressly provided, whenever forms are prescribed in
any Ordinance slight deviations therefrom, not affecting the substance or
calculated to mislead, shall not invalidate them.
Construction of amending Ordinance with amended Ordinance
55. Where an Ordinance amends another Ordinance the amending Ordinance
shall, so far as it is consistent with the tenor thereof, and unless the contrary
intention appears, be construed as one with the amended Ordinance.
Reprint of amended Ordinances
56. When any Ordinance is amended it shall be lawful for the Government
Printer with the authority of the Governor to print copies of the Ordinance with
all the necessary additions, omissions, substitutions and amendments effected by
the amending Ordinance or Ordinances and such copies shall be deemed to be
authentic copies of the Ordinance so amended.
Notification of enactment and sale of Subsidiary Legislation, etc.
57. Where any subsidiary legislation or other instrument of a public character
is required either expressly or by implication to be published or notified in the
Gazette, a notice in the Gazette that such subsidiary legislation or other
instrument has been made and of the place where copies thereof can be
purchased or perused shall be sufficient compliance with such requirement.

Evidence of signature of Governor
58. Whenever the fiat of the Governor is necessary before any prosecution or
action is commenced, any document purporting to bear the fiat of the Governor
shall be received as prima facie evidence in any proceeding without proof being
given that the signature to such fiat is that of the Governor.
Saving rights of the Crown
59. No Ordinance shall in any manner whatsoever affect the right of the
Crown unless it is therein expressly stated, or unless it appears by necessary
implication, that the Crown is bound thereby.
Ordinance binding on Crown
60. This Ordinance shall be binding on the Crown.

SUBSIDIARY LEGISLATION
MADE UNDER THE INTERPRETATION ORDINANCE
STANDARD TIME DECLARATIONS – SECTION 7
These notices are temporary in nature and are omitted from this revised edition.
The notices have generally been made by Legal Notice to keep the Turks and Caicos
Islands standard time in the same time zone as the Eastern United States of America—
with Daylight Saving Time being the same as the Daylight Saving Time in the Eastern
United States of America.
................................
CHANGE OF TITLE NOTICES – SECTION 47
The change of title Notices that have been made, have had their effect and are omitted
from this revised edition, but their effect has been shown in the laws.
................................
REPRINT OF LEGISLATION NOTICES – SECTION 55
The Notices authorising the Reprint of Legislation have had their effect and are omitted
from this revised edition.
